one_line_takeaway: SEAgent reframes diverse LLM-agent attacks (prompt injection, RAG poisoning, confused deputy, untrusted agents) as instances of a single formally-defined privilege-escalation problem and mitigates them with a SELinux-inspired ABAC/MAC layer over a per-round information-flow graph, achieving 0% ASR on InjecAgent and AgentDojo while IsolateGPT still leaks up to 54.55% ASR under Hub-level RAG poisoning and imposes far higher latency and FP rates.
key_finding: SEAgent achieves 0% ASR across all attack categories in both InjecAgent (App Compromise, App Data Stealing, RAG Poisoning sub-categories) and AgentDojo (Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace, Overall 0.00%), versus naive-agent ASR up to 80.00% (Physical harm, RAG poisoning, InjecAgent) and 84.13% (Slack, AgentDojo), and versus IsolateGPT ASR up to 54.55% on Hub-level RAG poisoning (Physical harm) and non-zero (up to 4.71%) even on Spoke-level RAG poisoning, plus 0.82% overall residual ASR on AgentDojo. On reconstructed API-Bank, SEAgent matches or exceeds naive-agent correctness (e.g., 74.73% vs. 70.33% for single-tool tasks) while IsolateGPT's correctness drops over 20 points on multi-tool tasks (e.g., 37.32% at 2 tools) and shows a high FP rate (18.31% at 2 tools) versus SEAgent's 0% FP at 1-2 tools; IsolateGPT also more than doubles execution time (e.g., 64.42s vs. 15.54s naive vs. 12.79s SEAgent at >=3 tools). On the original multi-turn API-Bank protocol (with SEMemory enabled), naive-agent average correctness (72.97%) is only modestly higher than SEAgent's (68.29%), at added token/latency cost attributed to API-Bank's short-context fixed overhead of SEMemory. On the multi-agent AWS benchmark, SEAgent improves Overall GSR over P2PEnv (74.24% vs. 71.97% Travel; 63.11% vs. 52.46% Mortgage), reduces token usage substantially (3180.94 vs. 4710.83 Travel; 3171.90 vs. 5198.62 Mortgage, i.e., roughly 32-39% reduction), and keeps execution time on par with baseline (44.38s vs. 45.85s Travel; 33.99s vs. 29.68s Mortgage), with policy-check latency negligible (avg. 0.00586s Travel, 0.00307s Mortgage).
core_contribution: The paper (1) formalizes LLM-based agent systems as a state-transition model over agents, tools, users, and RAG databases, and uses this model to define 'privilege escalation' as any agent invocation action outside the oracle-defined minimal action set needed to fulfill a user's query; (2) derives five attack vectors from this formal model (direct prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, RAG poisoning, confused-deputy attacks via inter-agent messages, and unauthorized actions by unverified third-party agents in multi-agent systems/MAS), and demonstrates concrete successful attacks against three state-of-the-art defenses -- SecAlign (prompt injection), IsolateGPT (RAG poisoning via both Hub- and Spoke-level injection, including an anonymous-Spoke permission-check gap), and CaMeL (argument-manipulation bypass of its dual-LLM plan-then-execute design) -- plus a novel confused-deputy attack against the AIOS-AutoGen multi-agent framework (also validated as a proof-of-concept on standard AutoGen and AIOS-MetaGPT); and (3) proposes SEAgent, a mandatory access control (MAC) framework built on attribute-based access control (ABAC) that maintains a live 'System View' directed graph of agent/tool/database information flows per execution round, labels each subject (tool, agent, RAG database) with security attributes (Object, Action, Sensitivity, Privacy, Integrity per Table 1), matches graph subpaths against a Policy Database of SELinux-inspired Goal/Path/Rule policies via a Decision Engine, and preserves cross-round context via a SEMemory module that performs selective recall instead of raw context accumulation.
threat_model: Adversary has white-box knowledge of the system architecture and toolset T but cannot directly modify model weights or agent runtime execution. The adversary can (a) manipulate response actions -- inject malicious content into user queries (acting as a malicious/guest user), poison tool return results, or poison RAG retrieval content -- and (b) in multi-agent settings, deploy or compromise untrusted third-party agents to control their generated inter-agent messages and tool invocations via crafted system prompts. Three explicit system assumptions scope the model: tools come from a trusted SDK; agents do not escalate privileges unless prompted by adversarial input; and single-agent systems' primary agent is trusted, while in MAS third-party agents may carry untrusted system prompts.
limitations: The paper explicitly discusses two self-identified limitations: (1) subject labeling is currently a hybrid LLM+human process rather than fully automated, and the authors note future work should explore fine-tuned models for automated policy/label generation as attack vectors evolve; (2) SEAgent relies on statically assigned security attributes for agents/tools/databases that cannot be updated dynamically at runtime, though the authors argue this static design trades adaptability for deterministic, auditable enforcement suited to high-assurance settings. Additional limitations evident from the evaluation itself: the oracle-defined minimal action set T_q underlying the privilege-escalation definition is stated to be generally unknown in advance, making the formal definition inherently approximated in practice by policy heuristics; untrusted-agent/confused-deputy defense is evaluated only via case studies (not a large-scale benchmark) due to lack of existing benchmarks for that attack class; on the multi-turn API-Bank protocol SEMemory increases token usage and, in the two-tool case, actually reduces correctness relative to the naive agent (50.00% vs. 74.39%); and in the AWS multi-agent benchmark, tools are LLM-simulated without real backends, so labeling/policy enforcement was skipped and only runtime overhead (not security) was measured there.

datasets_used: InjecAgent (80 tools; extended by the authors with a RAG-poisoning variant)
AgentDojo (74 tools across Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace task suites)
API-Bank (52 tools, 214 task instances; evaluated via both a reconstructed single-round protocol and the original multi-turn protocol)
AWS Benchmark (Travel scenario: 9 agents; Mortgage scenario: 5 agents; 30 task instances each; Software scenario excluded due to topology complexity)
quantitative_results: SEAgent: 0% ASR across all attack categories on InjecAgent (App Compromise, App Data Stealing, RAG Poisoning) and AgentDojo (Overall 0.00%)
Naive agent ASR: up to 80.00% (Physical harm/RAG poisoning, InjecAgent); 84.13% (Slack suite, AgentDojo); Overall 39.14% (AgentDojo)
IsolateGPT ASR: up to 54.55% (Hub-level RAG poisoning, Physical harm, InjecAgent) and up to 4.71% residual on Spoke-level RAG poisoning; Overall 0.82% (AgentDojo)
Reconstructed API-Bank correctness (1-tool): naive 70.33%, IsolateGPT 53.95%, SEAgent 74.73%
Reconstructed API-Bank correctness (>=3 tools): naive 63.43%, IsolateGPT 37.25%, SEAgent 67.91%
Reconstructed API-Bank FP rate (2-tool): IsolateGPT 18.31%, SEAgent 0%
Reconstructed API-Bank execution time (>=3 tools): naive 15.54s, IsolateGPT 64.42s, SEAgent 12.79s
Original multi-turn API-Bank average correctness across categories: naive agent 72.97%, SEAgent 68.29%
AWS Travel: Overall GSR P2PEnv 71.97% vs. SEAgent 74.24%; token usage 4710.83 vs. 3180.94; execution time 45.85s vs. 44.38s
AWS Mortgage: Overall GSR P2PEnv 52.46% vs. SEAgent 63.11%; token usage 5198.62 vs. 3171.90; execution time 29.68s vs. 33.99s
baselines_compared: SecAlign (fine-tuning-based defense, attacked in case study)
CaMeL (dual-LLM plan-then-execute defense, attacked in case study)
